note_data.title: Light
keyPoints: Magnets have north and south poles - like poles repel, opposite poles attract
Magnetic materials (iron, steel, cobalt, nickel) are always attracted to magnets regardless of pole
Non-magnetic materials (glass, plastic) experience no force from magnets
Magnetic materials can be magnetized by stroking with magnets, hammering in magnetic fields, or placing in coils with direct current
Temporary magnets (soft iron) lose magnetism when field removed, permanent magnets retain it
Electromagnets are temporary magnets made from soft iron in coiled wire with direct current
Magnetic field lines show direction (north to south) and strength (closer lines = stronger field)
Electric charge is measured in coulombs - like charges repel, opposite charges attract
Conductors allow charge flow through free moving particles, insulators impede charge flow
Insulators gain electrostatic charge when rubbed together due to electron transfer

definitions.definition: A material or object that produces a magnetic field
A region in which a magnetic pole experiences the force from another magnet or magnetic material
A temporary magnet made when soft iron is placed within coiled wire with direct current running through it
Measured in coulombs and can be positive or negative
Materials which allow electric charge to flow through them because they have charged particles that are free to move
Materials which impede the flow of electric charge because they don't have charged particles that are free to move

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Give a sequence of events or properties without explanation
Always use ruler for straight lines, label all parts, show angles clearly

modelAnswer.question: A ray of light travels from air into glass. Explain why the ray bends towards the normal. [3 marks]
modelAnswer.answer: Light slows down when it enters the glass because glass is denser than air. This change in speed causes the light ray to refract. The ray bends towards the normal because it is travelling from a less dense medium to a more dense medium.
markBreakdown: Mark 1: Light slows down in glass
Mark 2: Because glass is denser than air
Mark 3: Ray bends towards normal when going from less dense to more dense medium

commonMistakes: Thinking magnetic materials are only attracted to one specific pole - they're attracted to both poles
Confusing temporary and permanent magnets - temporary magnets lose magnetism when the field is removed
Drawing magnetic field lines pointing from south to north instead of north to south
Thinking only positive charges move during electrostatic charging - only electrons (negative charge) transfer
examinerTips: Remember magnetic field strength is shown by spacing of field lines - closer together means stronger field
When plotting magnetic fields, always draw arrows pointing from north to south pole
Electromagnets are used for things that turn on/off, permanent magnets for things always on
formulas: refractive index = sin i / sin r
sin c = 1/n (where c is critical angle and n is refractive index)
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Ray diagram showing incident ray, reflected ray, refracted ray, and normal line (perpendicular to surface). All angles measured from normal. Include labels for angle of incidence, angle of reflection, and angle of refraction.
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P2 Q3 (4 marks) — they asked students to calculate power of daylight incident on a sensor with area 2.2 cm² and reading 950 W m⁻²
N/D 2022 P1 Q15 (1 mark) — multiple choice question on which property of light allows formation of shadows
M/J 2024 P2 Q7b (3 marks) — students had to explain why light intensity decreases with distance from a point source
N/D 2023 P1 Q18 (1 mark) — asked about the speed of light in different media compared to vacuum
M/J 2022 P2 Q5c (2 marks) — calculation of light energy converted from electrical energy in a filament lamp

workedExample.question: A light sensor has an area of 2.2 cm² and gives a reading of 950 W m⁻². Calculate the power of daylight incident on the sensor. (3 marks)
steps: Step 1: Convert area to m²: 2.2 cm² = 2.2 × 10⁻⁴ m²
Step 2: Use formula Power = Intensity × Area
Step 3: Substitute values: Power = 950 W m⁻² × 2.2 × 10⁻⁴ m²
Step 4: Calculate: Power = 0.209 W = 0.21 W (to 2 s.f.)
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark for correct area conversion, 1 mark for correct formula/method, 1 mark for correct final answer with appropriate significant figures

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates confused luminous and non-luminous objects' — always distinguish between objects that produce their own light versus those that reflect it
Examiner Report 2024: 'Students frequently forgot to convert cm² to m² in intensity calculations' — always check units are consistent before substituting into formulas

Examiner Report 2022: 'Common error was stating light speed changes in different media without explaining refraction occurs' — link speed changes to bending of light rays when answering refraction questions
note_data.keyDiagram: Ray diagram showing incident ray, reflected ray, and normal at 90° to reflecting surface. Must include: clearly labeled angles of incidence and reflection (measured from normal), arrows showing light direction, and dotted line for normal. For refraction diagrams, show rays bending toward normal when entering denser medium, away from normal when entering less dense medium.
note_data.quickRecap: Magnets have north and south poles with magnetic fields that can be visualized using plotting compasses or iron filings. Magnetic materials can be temporarily or permanently magnetized, with electromagnets being useful temporary magnets. Electric charges create forces between objects, with conductors allowing charge flow and insulators impeding it. Electrostatic charging occurs through electron transfer when insulators are rubbed together.
